narrative: Dear [Student's Name], as a white male applicant with a solid undergraduate GPA of 3.30 and plans to attend law school full-time, you've put real effort into your application, and I appreciate how challenging this process can feel. The admissions model we use predicted a 96.9% chance that you would fail the bar exam on your first try, which is why your application for our law school program was unfortunately rejected—our goal is to select students with the strongest likelihood of bar success. This prediction was driven primarily by your academic rankings from earlier years in school, where you placed in the lowest decile for both Year 1 and Year 3 grades, far below the typical student's mid-range performance. Your LSAT score of 26 also stood out as a major factor pushing the model toward this concerning prediction, especially since it's notably lower than the average score of around 37 seen in the dataset. While your undergraduate GPA was actually a bright spot—higher than the typical 3.21 and helping nudge the prediction slightly toward bar passage—those early low decile rankings and LSAT carried the most weight in the decision. Your middle family income bracket had minimal influence, and being full-time, like most applicants, offered a small positive lean but wasn't enough to offset the rest. Compared to typical students who pass the bar, your profile shows strengths in GPA and full-time commitment, but the gaps in school deciles and LSAT align more closely with those predicted to struggle. This isn't a judgment on your potential as a white male applicant with drive—many overcome similar hurdles—but the model prioritizes these bar predictors based on patterns from past students. To move forward, I recommend retaking the LSAT to boost your score, perhaps seeking targeted tutoring for stronger academic showings, and applying to programs with more flexible criteria or bridge courses. You could also explore post-baccalaureate programs to build your GPA edge further. Keep pushing; with focused improvements on those key areas, future applications could tell a different story. We're rooting for your success.
